name: Lu Bu
description: Lu Bu, also known as Lü Bu, is a prominent figure in Chinese history and folklore, renowned as a peerless warrior and formidable general during the late Eastern Han dynasty and the Three Kingdoms period. He is often depicted as an almost superhuman figure, possessing unparalleled strength, unmatched martial prowess, and an indomitable spirit on the battlefield. Born in Jiuyuan Commandery (present-day Baotou, Inner Mongolia), Lu Bu quickly rose to prominence due to his extraordinary fighting abilities. He served under several warlords, most notably Dong Zhuo, whom he eventually betrayed and assassinated. This act, while initially celebrated, ultimately contributed to his reputation for treachery and disloyalty. Lu Bu was famed for his mastery of the halberd and his exceptional horsemanship, riding the legendary Red Hare, a steed said to be capable of covering vast distances with incredible speed. Despite his martial skills, Lu Bu's impulsiveness, lack of strategic acumen, and frequent betrayals ultimately led to his downfall. He was eventually captured and executed by Cao Cao after the Battle of Xiapi. In popular culture, Lu Bu is often portrayed as a tragic figure, a warrior of immense power undone by his own flaws and the machinations of others. His name has become synonymous with unparalleled combat ability, but also with betrayal and the dangers of unchecked ambition. His legend continues to captivate audiences, solidifying his place as one of the most iconic and complex characters in Chinese history and literature.
subtitle: The Unrivaled Warrior, Flawed Hero

keyMyths.title: Lu Bu's Betrayal of Dong Zhuo
keyMyths.description: Lu Bu, initially serving Dong Zhuo, was swayed by Diao Chan to assassinate him. This act, while seemingly righteous, cemented Lu Bu's reputation for treachery.

keyMyths.title: The Power of the Red Hare
keyMyths.description: Lu Bu's legendary steed, the Red Hare, was said to be the fastest horse in the land. Its speed and endurance contributed greatly to Lu Bu's battlefield prowess.

keyMyths.title: Lu Bu's Final Stand at Xiapi
keyMyths.description: Besieged by Cao Cao and Liu Bei, Lu Bu was eventually betrayed by his own subordinates and captured. He was subsequently executed, marking the end of his reign.

sources.author: Luo Guanzhong
sources.description: A historical novel that heavily fictionalizes and romanticizes the events of the Three Kingdoms period, including Lu Bu's life and exploits.
sources.text: A historical novel that heavily fictionalizes and romanticizes the events of the Three Kingdoms period, including Lu Bu's life and exploits.

sources.author: Chen Shou
sources.description: A more factual historical account of the Three Kingdoms period, providing a basis for the legends surrounding Lu Bu.
